name: Long Division — Share the Big, Then the Small
whatIsIt: Long division shares BIG numbers fairly by dealing out the biggest place first: share the tens, trade any leftovers into ones, then share the ones. 72 ÷ 3: share 7 tens → 2 tens each (1 ten left); trade it → 12 ones; share → 4 each. Answer: 24 each. Sometimes a REMAINDER stays — the bit that can't be shared fairly.
whyNeeded: Divya's card-dealing works for 12 laddus, but for 72 — or 728 — dealing one at a time takes all day! Long division is the grown-up fairness machine: it shares hundreds, then tens, then ones, using nothing but tables and subtraction you already own.
realLifeUses: 96 students into 4 equal houses for sports day
85 laddus packed 6 to a box — with some left over!
125 pages to read equally over 5 days

vocabulary.meaning: Sharing place by place: biggest place first, trade leftovers down.
What's left when the total won't share evenly: 85 ÷ 6 = 14 remainder 1.
Leftover tens become ones (1 ten → 10 ones) — regrouping again!

story.title: Birbal Divides the Reward
story.text: Three helpers earned ₹72 together and asked wise Birbal to split it fairly. Birbal shared the TENS first: 7 ten-rupee coins among 3 is 2 tens each, with 1 ten left over. He traded that leftover ten for 10 one-rupee coins, making 12 ones, and shared those: 4 each. 'So each helper gets ₹24. Share the big first, then trade down to the small.'
story.extractedProblem: Share ₹72 (7 tens + 2 ones) equally among 3 helpers.
story.answerInStory: ₹24 each — 2 tens each with 1 ten left, traded to 12 ones, then 4 ones each.

visual.caption: Share tens first (2 each, 1 left) → trade the leftover ten into 10 ones → share ones (4 each). 72 ÷ 3 = 24.

teachingGallery.title: Long division — biggest part first
teachingGallery.note: Share the tens first, then bring down the ones and share again.

standardMethod.summary: Divide, multiply, subtract, bring down — place by place from the left.
steps: DIVIDE the tens: 7 ÷ 3 → 2 (write 2 above the tens).
MULTIPLY & SUBTRACT: 2×3 = 6; 7−6 = 1 ten left.
BRING DOWN the ones: leftover 1 ten + 2 ones = 12.
DIVIDE again: 12 ÷ 3 = 4. Answer 24. Check: 24 × 3 = 72 ✓

alternateMethods.name: Note-and-coin sharing (concrete)
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To SEE what every step of the ladder means.
steps: Build the number with ten-notes and one-coins.
Deal notes equally; trade any leftover note into 10 coins.
Deal the coins. What each person holds IS the answer.
alternateMethods.example: 96 ÷ 4: nine notes → 2 each (1 left) → trade → 16 coins → 4 each → 24.

alternateMethods.name: Chunking (repeated friendly jumps)
steps: Grab big friendly chunks of the divisor.
72 ÷ 3: take 3×20 = 60 (chunk of 20 each), 12 remains.

workedExamples.problem: 96 students form 4 equal sports houses. How many per house?
steps: Tens: 9 ÷ 4 → 2 each (write 2), 2×4=8, 9−8 = 1 ten left.
Trade & bring down: 1 ten + 6 ones = 16.
Ones: 16 ÷ 4 = 4. Answer: 24 per house.

workedExamples.problem: 85 laddus are packed 6 to a box. Boxes filled, and laddus left over?
steps: Tens: 8 ÷ 6 → 1 (1×6=6, 8−6=2 left). Bring down 5 → 25.
Ones: 25 ÷ 6 → 4 (4×6=24), remainder 1.
14 boxes filled, 1 laddu left over — the remainder!
Check: 14×6 + 1 = 85 ✓ (quotient × divisor + remainder = total)

trickPractice.trick: Share the big, then the small
trickPractice.intro: Divide the tens first, carry what's left to the ones — the number never overwhelms you.

hintLadder: Share the TENS of 84 first among 4.
Then bring down the ones and share again.
Share the TENS of 69 first among 3.
Then bring down the ones and share again.
Share the TENS of 96 first among 6.
Then bring down the ones and share again.
Share the TENS of 75 first among 5.
Then bring down the ones and share again.
Share the TENS of 92 first among 4.
Then bring down the ones and share again.
Share the TENS of 78 first among 6.
Then bring down the ones and share again.
Share the TENS of 85 first among 5.
Then bring down the ones and share again.
Share the TENS of 72 first among 3.
Then bring down the ones and share again.

commonMistakes.mistake: Sharing the tens, then IGNORING the leftover ten (72÷3 = '2 tens each and 2÷3... umm 0' → 20).
commonMistakes.fix: Leftover tens never vanish — they TRADE DOWN! 1 leftover ten = 10 ones joins the ones queue: 10+2 = 12 ones to share. Divya never wastes a note!

commonMistakes.mistake: Leaving a remainder bigger than the divisor (85 ÷ 6 = 13 remainder 7).
commonMistakes.fix: If the remainder is ≥ the divisor, another full share is hiding in it! 7 holds one more 6 → quotient 14, remainder 1. The remainder must always be SMALLER than the divisor.

commonMistakes.mistake: Never verifying with the twin multiplication.
commonMistakes.fix: Division's bodyguard: quotient × divisor (+ remainder) must rebuild the start. 24×3 = 72 ✓. Ten seconds, total certainty.

easy.q: ₹48 shared equally between 2 siblings. How much each?

medium.q: 96 mangoes shared among 4 baskets. How many per basket?
85 laddus, 6 per box. How many FULL boxes? (Watch the remainder!)

challenge.q: Ravi answered 85 ÷ 6 = '13 remainder 7'. Divya frowns instantly. Why?

options.label: A remainder of 7 still holds a whole 6 — it must be smaller than the divisor
Nothing wrong — big remainders are fine
He should have no remainder at all

challenge.answer: A remainder of 7 still holds a whole 6 — it must be smaller than the divisor

challenge.q: 125 pages over 5 days, equal reading. Pages per day? (Try chunking: 5×20 first.)

hintLadder: If it's bigger, what's hiding inside it?

teachBackPrompt: Run Divya's money machine for someone at home: share ₹72 among 3 with real (or paper) notes and coins — deal the tens, TRADE the leftover note, deal the coins — then show the same steps written as long division.

revisionCard.summary: Long division = share the BIG place first, TRADE leftovers down, share again. 72÷3: tens (2 each, 1 left) → trade → 12 ones → 4 each = 24. Remainder always SMALLER than the divisor. Bodyguard: quotient×divisor(+remainder) = start!

gameMission.title: The Money Machine Marathon
gameMission.brief: Grandma's gift envelopes keep arriving — ₹72, ₹96, ₹85! Run each through Divya's machine: deal, trade, deal, and box up remainders. One oversized remainder and the machine jams — keep it purring!

realLifeProject: Share a real pile: take 50-80 small things (buttons, beans) and share among 3 or 4 family members using deal-trade-deal with tens-bundles. Write the long division that matches what your hands did — including any remainder!

vedicMethod.name: Vedic: quick division by 9 (Nikhilam)
vedicMethod.whenToUse: To divide a 2-digit number by 9 in your head (9 is one less than the base 10).
steps: The first digit is the quotient to start: for 23 ÷ 9, quotient starts at 2.
Add the two digits for the remainder: 2 + 3 = 5.
So 23 ÷ 9 = 2 remainder 5. (Check: 9 × 2 + 5 = 23 ✓.)
If the remainder reaches 9 or more, carry one more into the quotient.

mentalMathMethod.name: Chunking (subtract big chunks)
mentalMathMethod.whenToUse: For bigger divisions in your head.
steps: Subtract easy big chunks of the divisor (like 10× or 5×).
Keep track of how many groups you took.
Add the chunks to get the quotient.

rememberIt.hook: DMSB: Divide, Multiply, Subtract, Bring down (repeat)
rememberIt.unpack: 'Does McDonald's Sell Burgers?' Divide, Multiply, Subtract, Bring down the next digit, then repeat until no digits are left.

studentQuestions.q: I keep forgetting the steps - is there an order?
studentQuestions.a: DMSB: Divide, Multiply, Subtract, Bring down. Repeat. 'Does McDonald's Sell Burgers?'

studentQuestions.q: What if a digit is too small to divide?
studentQuestions.a: Put a 0 in the answer for that place and bring down the next digit to make a bigger number.
